content.text: Summarisation is the task of producing a shorter representation of a longer text that preserves its most important content. For LLM applications, summarisation ranges from simple single-document distillation to multi-document synthesis, live streaming transcripts, and hierarchical reduction of million-word corpora. The implementation pattern, output format, and quality criteria all depend on the source length, the audience, and how the summary will be used downstream.

attrs.alt: Three summarisation strategies: (1) Single document — fits in context window, one request to LLM, direct summary. (2) Map-Reduce — chunk the document, summarise each chunk in parallel, reduce partial summaries to final summary. (3) Incremental — maintain running summary, update it as new chunks arrive, suitable for live streams. Output formats: executive summary (paragraph), bullet points, structured JSON, chain of density (iterative densification). Faithfulness to the source is the primary quality dimension.

content.text: When the source text fits inside the context window (up to ~150k words for 200k-token models), the simplest approach is the best: send the full document and ask for a summary. The model has access to the whole text at once and can weigh relevance globally.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def summarise(
    text: str,
    style: str = "executive",  # "executive" | "bullets" | "detailed"
    max_words: int = 150,
) -> str:
    style_instructions = {
        "executive": f"Write a {max_words}-word executive summary. One paragraph. Focus on decisions, impact, and key findings. No bullet points.",
        "bullets":   f"Summarise in {max_words // 20} bullet points. Each point must be self-contained. No preamble.",
        "detailed":  f"Write a {max_words}-word detailed summary preserving technical nuance. Maintain section structure.",
    }
    system = (
        "You are a precise summarisation assistant. "
        "Only include information explicitly stated in the source text. "
        "Do not infer, speculate, or add outside knowledge."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=system,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": f"{style_instructions[style]}\n\nDocument:\n{text}"},
        ],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

article = """
The city council approved a $2.4 billion infrastructure plan at Tuesday's meeting.
The package includes $800M for public transit upgrades, $600M for road repair,
$500M for broadband expansion in underserved areas, and $500M for flood-resilience
projects along the river delta. Implementation begins Q1 next year.
Council member Davis dissented, citing insufficient environmental review.
"""

print(summarise(article, style="bullets"))
# • City council approved $2.4B infrastructure plan
# • $800M: public transit | $600M: roads | $500M: broadband | $500M: flood-resilience
# • Implementation starts Q1 next year
# • Council member Davis dissented over insufficient environmental review

For documents that exceed the context window, split them into chunks, summarise each chunk independently (map), then combine the partial summaries into a final summary (reduce). The map step can run in parallel — all chunks simultaneously — which minimises wall-clock time.

import asyncio
import anthropic

async_client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_words: int = 2000) -> list[str]:
    """Split on word boundaries into chunks of ~chunk_words words."""
    words = text.split()
    return [
        " ".join(words[i : i + chunk_words])
        for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_words)
    ]

async def summarise_chunk(chunk: str, index: int) -> str:
    """MAP: summarise a single chunk."""
    response = await async_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        system="Summarise the passage in 3–5 sentences. Only include information from the passage.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": chunk}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

async def reduce_summaries(partial_summaries: list[str]) -> str:
    """REDUCE: combine partial summaries into a final coherent summary."""
    combined = "\n\n".join(
        f"[Part {i+1}]\n{s}" for i, s in enumerate(partial_summaries)
    )
    response = await async_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system="Synthesise the partial summaries below into a single coherent summary. Remove redundancy. Maintain key facts.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": combined}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

async def map_reduce_summarise(text: str) -> str:
    chunks = chunk_text(text, chunk_words=2000)
    print(f"Splitting into {len(chunks)} chunks...")

    # MAP: all chunks summarised concurrently
    partial = await asyncio.gather(*[
        summarise_chunk(chunk, i) for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks)
    ])

    # REDUCE: combine into final summary
    if len(partial) == 1:
        return partial[0]  # single chunk — skip reduce
    return await reduce_summaries(partial)

# long_document = open("report.txt").read()
# summary = asyncio.run(map_reduce_summarise(long_document))
# print(summary)

For live data streams (meeting transcripts, news feeds, log files) where text arrives continuously, maintain a running summary and update it as each new chunk arrives. The summary stays current and bounded in size regardless of total stream length.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def update_rolling_summary(current_summary: str, new_chunk: str, max_words: int = 200) -> str:
    """Incorporate a new chunk into the running summary."""
    if not current_summary:
        # First chunk: generate initial summary
        prompt = f"Summarise the following in {max_words} words:\n\n{new_chunk}"
    else:
        prompt = (
            f"Current summary (from earlier content):\n{current_summary}\n\n"
            f"New content to incorporate:\n{new_chunk}\n\n"
            f"Update the summary to include the new content. Keep it under {max_words} words. "
            f"Preserve all important information from both the current summary and new content."
        )

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=max_words * 2,  # generous budget; model typically stays within instructions
        system="You are a precise summarisation assistant. Only include information from the provided text.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Simulate a meeting transcript arriving in segments
transcript_segments = [
    "Alice opened the meeting. The Q3 revenue came in at $4.2M, up 12% YoY.",
    "Bob presented the product roadmap. Three features ship in Q4: dark mode, export, and API v2.",
    "Carol raised concerns about the support backlog — currently 340 open tickets, up from 180 last quarter.",
    "The team agreed to hire two additional support engineers. Budget approved by Alice.",
]

summary = ""
for segment in transcript_segments:
    summary = update_rolling_summary(summary, segment)
    print(f"Running summary: {summary[:120]}...\n")

For summaries consumed by downstream systems (database storage, dashboard display, search indexing), structured JSON output is more reliable than free text. Define the schema and instruct the model to populate it.

import anthropic
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class MeetingSummary:
    title: str
    date: str | None
    attendees: list[str]
    key_decisions: list[str]
    action_items: list[dict]  # [{"owner": str, "task": str, "due": str|None}]
    next_meeting: str | None

MEETING_SYSTEM = """\
Extract meeting summary information from the transcript.
Output valid JSON only — no preamble or explanation:
{
  "title": "<inferred meeting title>",
  "date": "<ISO 8601 date or null>",
  "attendees": ["<name>", ...],
  "key_decisions": ["<decision>", ...],
  "action_items": [
    {"owner": "<name>", "task": "<task>", "due": "<date or null>"}
  ],
  "next_meeting": "<date or null>"
}
Only include information explicitly stated in the transcript."""

def summarise_meeting(transcript: str) -> MeetingSummary:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=MEETING_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": transcript}],
    )
    data = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return MeetingSummary(**data)

transcript = """
Date: June 27, 2026. Attendees: Alice (CEO), Bob (PM), Carol (Support Lead).
Alice: Q3 revenue $4.2M, up 12% YoY.
Bob: Shipping dark mode, export, and API v2 in Q4.
Carol: Support backlog is 340 tickets, up from 180. We need help.
Alice: Approved: hire 2 support engineers. Bob to post JDs by July 4.
Next meeting: July 11.
"""

result = summarise_meeting(transcript)
print(result.key_decisions)
# ['Hire 2 additional support engineers']
print(result.action_items)
# [{'owner': 'Bob', 'task': 'Post job descriptions for support engineers', 'due': '2026-07-04'}]

Chain-of-density (Adams et al., 2023) is a prompting technique that iteratively densifies a summary: start with a short, sparse summary; in each iteration, add more missing entities and detail while keeping the word count the same. The result is a highly informative summary that reads naturally.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def chain_of_density(article: str, iterations: int = 3, target_words: int = 80) -> str:
    """Iteratively densify a summary without increasing length."""
    system = f"""\
You will iteratively produce more information-dense summaries.
Each iteration: identify missing key entities from the article,
then rewrite the summary to include them WITHOUT increasing length ({target_words} words).

Output JSON: {{"summary": "<the dense summary>", "added_entities": ["entity1", ...]}}"""

    summary = f"Write an initial {target_words}-word summary of the article. Be broad and sparse."
    article_prompt = f"Article:\n{article}\n\n{summary}"

    current_summary = ""

    for i in range(iterations):
        if i == 0:
            prompt = article_prompt
        else:
            prompt = (
                f"Article:\n{article}\n\n"
                f"Current summary (iteration {i}):\n{current_summary}\n\n"
                f"Now densify: add missing key entities, keep ≤{target_words} words."
            )

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=256,
            system=system,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        result = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
        current_summary = result["summary"]
        print(f"Iteration {i+1}: {current_summary[:80]}...")
        print(f"  Added: {result.get('added_entities', [])}\n")

    return current_summary

The primary quality risk in summarisation is hallucination: the model adds facts not in the source text. This is especially dangerous for legal, medical, or financial documents where a fabricated claim could be taken as authoritative. Two techniques reduce it:
include "Only include information explicitly stated in the source. Do not infer or add outside knowledge." in the system prompt.
after generating the summary, ask the model to quote the source sentence for each claim. If a claim cannot be grounded in a quote, it is likely hallucinated.
Not specifying the target audience in the prompt.
"Summarise this" produces an average summary. "Summarise for a non-technical executive who needs to approve a budget" produces a much more appropriate one. Always specify who will read the summary.
Using map-reduce when the document fits in context.
Chunking introduces seam artefacts: the model cannot consider cross-chunk relationships. Single-document summarisation is always higher quality when feasible.

An LLM-generated summary of a legal contract or medical record that contains a single hallucinated fact can cause significant harm. Always verify faithfulness for documents where accuracy is non-negotiable.
Evaluating summary quality by reading only the summary.
A coherent, well-written summary can still be unfaithful or miss critical information. Evaluation must compare the summary against the source, not just judge the summary in isolation.
